title: Climbing Stairs
problem_statement: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can either climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?

examples.explanation:
1. 1 step + 1 step + 1 step
2. 1 step + 2 steps
3. 2 steps + 1 step

intuition: This is a classic Fibonacci sequence problem where the number of ways to reach step n is the sum of ways to reach n-1 and n-2.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: Direct recursion: for each step, choose to take 1 or 2 steps. Base cases: 0 steps = 1 way (do nothing), negative = 0.
approaches.why_not: Exponential time due to redundant calculations of overlapping subproblems. Fails for n > 20.
interview_commentary: This is the most straightforward way to think about it — at each step, you have two choices: take 1 or take 2.
The recursion tree will have a lot of overlap — for example, climbStairs(3) calls climbStairs(2) and climbStairs(1), and climbStairs(2) calls the same climbStairs(1) again.
This is a signal to memoize or use DP to avoid recomputing the same subproblems.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion as brute force, but cache results in an array. Use memo[n] to store ways for n.
approaches.why_not: Still uses recursion stack, though shallower. Bottom-up is iterative and avoids stack altogether.
interview_commentary: To fix the exponential time, I'll add memoization. I'll use an array memo where memo[i] stores the number of ways to reach i.
Before recursing, check if we've already computed it. This brings time down to O(n) since each n is computed once.
Space is O(n) for the memo array, plus O(n) for the recursion stack in the worst case.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Iteratively build the solution from base cases. Use two variables to track the last two values, updating them in a loop.
approaches.why_not: This is optimal. No further optimizations needed for this problem.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up DP starts from the base cases and builds up. Here, a and b represent ways for n-2 and n-1.
Each iteration computes ways for the current n by summing the previous two.
This uses constant space since we only need the last two values, and it's iterative so no recursion stack.

interview_qa.q: Why is this a Fibonacci problem?
interview_qa.a: Because ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2), matching the Fibonacci recurrence where each number is the sum of the two preceding ones.
ways(0) = 1 (one way to be at the bottom), ways(1) = 1 (one step).

interview_qa.q: Can you generalize this to k steps instead of 1 or 2?
interview_qa.a: Yes, if you can take 1 to k steps, ways(n) = sum of ways(n-1) + ways(n-2) + ... + ways(n-k). The recurrence becomes ways(n) = sum_{i=1 to k} ways(n-i) for n >= 0.
It stores results of subproblems so we don't recompute them. For example, ways(3) computes ways(2) and ways(1), and ways(2) computes ways(1) and ways(0), but ways(1) is only computed once.
